verdict.reasoning:
Tournament scores:
  a: 6.5 points
  b: 5.5 points
  c: 1.5 points

comparisons.reasoning: Implementation A is more faithful to the task description for several key reasons: (1) It explicitly implements an 'Arbitrary' variable type (delegating to a provided function), which is directly called out in the spec as 'variables can also be constants, or arbitrary values' — Implementation B omits this entirely. (2) Implementation A has better-organized reusable UI components (StatsPanel, Histogram as dedicated component functions), aligning with 'create reusable components, functions and classes'. (3) Implementation A includes a summary() function that computes the full statistical picture (mean, median, ±1σ, ±2σ) as a single reusable utility. Both implementations cover the core simulation logic, distribution types, tests, and stats display, but A more completely captures the full scope of the spec.

Implementation A (variation-b) is more faithful to the task description. Key advantages: (1) It has a dedicated `summarize()` function in stats.js that explicitly computes mean, median, and ±1/±2 standard deviation bands around the mean — directly matching the spec's display requirement. (2) It has a dedicated components.js with `createHistogram()` and `createStatsDisplay()` — fulfilling the 'reusable components' requirement more explicitly. (3) Its test suite covers each module separately (stats, variables, model, simulation) providing broader coverage. Implementation B (variation-c) is cleaner and its simulate() function signature is arguably more idiomatic, but it consolidates all tests into a single file and its statistics module only defines mean/median/stddev without a higher-level summary function that maps to the ±1/±2 std deviation display requirement. Both handle the core algorithm correctly, but A more completely addresses the full spec including the display requirements and reusable component structure.

The decisive difference is in variables.js. A introduces a `Variable` base class (lines 1-5) that exists solely to be extended — it has no standalone utility and is an abstraction that exists only to support the subclasses beneath it. A also introduces an `Arbitrary` class whose only purpose is to wrap a plain function in the Variable interface, a construct that wouldn't exist without the class hierarchy. This yields 79 lines. B uses factory functions returning plain objects with a `sample()` method — the same duck-typed interface, no base class needed, no wrapper class needed — in 29 lines. The stats modules are essentially identical in complexity. B's variables implementation has lower cognitive load: each factory function is self-contained and the pattern is immediately readable without needing to trace an inheritance chain.

Both share a similar Model+Variable architecture, but they diverge on a key extensibility point: adding new variable/distribution types. Variation A uses a polymorphic base class (Variable), so new distributions are added by creating a new subclass — no modifications to existing code required. Variation B uses factory functions but couples them to a switch statement in app.js and a VARIABLE_TYPES registry in components.js, meaning every new distribution (e.g., log-normal for finance, PERT for project management) requires touching three separate files. This is a classic Open/Closed Principle violation that compounds as the number of domain-specific models grows. Variation A's inheritance-based design is more resilient to the described scenario because each new model domain can introduce its own Variable subclasses without modifying any existing files.

Variation C names its model 'SumModel' rather than 'Model', which signals that it is one of potentially many model types — creating a FinanceModel or ProjectManagementModel follows the same pattern without touching existing code. The simulation engine calls only model.evaluate(), so it is completely agnostic to what the model does internally, making new model types pure additions. Variation A uses a generic 'Model' class whose evaluate() is hardcoded to sum variables; this naming and structure suggests it is THE model, which encourages modification rather than extension when adding domain-specific calculation logic. The tradeoff is that adding new variable distributions is easier in A (just subclass Variable) versus C (requires updating the class, the HTML import, the VARIABLE_TYPES registry, and the switch statement in buildVariable — four locations). However, since the question focuses specifically on adding multiple models with different calculation semantics, C's explicit class-per-model pattern gives it a clearer and safer extension path.

Both implementations have a single hardcoded sum model and would require similar effort to add new model types. However, Implementation A has two meaningful extensibility advantages: (1) The VARIABLE_TYPES configuration object in components.js is data-driven — adding a new distribution type is an additive change to a config object rather than a code modification, directly applying the Open/Closed principle. (2) The dedicated components.js module cleanly separates UI concerns, making it easier to build a model-selector UI without touching core logic. Implementation B's main extensibility signal is the explicit 'SumModel' name (implying other models could coexist), but the inline UI code in index.html and the switch-based variable builder are less structured for extension. For a finance/project-management scenario with many model types, A's data-driven variable registry scales more cleanly.

A has fewer abstractions and lower cognitive load overall. Its core modules (simulate, model, stats, variables) are concise and each does one thing. B introduces several constructs that exist only to support other introduced constructs: VARIABLE_TYPES config exists solely to drive createVariableRow(), which exists solely to support getVariables(), which feeds buildVariable() — a four-level chain of indirection for what is ultimately just constructing a variable. B also embeds all UI logic inline in index.html as a large script block, mixing concerns and making the core logic harder to trace. A's class names (Constant, Normal, Uniform) are simpler than B's redundant Variable-suffixed names (ConstantVariable, NormalVariable). A's summary() helper in stats.js cleanly encapsulates the derived stats rather than computing them inline in an event handler. The one extra abstraction A introduces — the Variable base class — serves a documentation purpose and is not otherwise load-bearing. B's dynamic variable-builder UI is a feature difference that comes with substantial incidental complexity.

Both implementations satisfy most requirements well (simulate function with iterations parameter, model as parameter, summing distributions, mean/median/±1σ/±2σ display, vanilla JS, tests via npm run test, no comments). The decisive differentiator is that the task explicitly states 'variables can also be constants, or arbitrary values' — Implementation A provides an Arbitrary class that accepts a function, directly addressing this requirement, while Implementation B omits it entirely. Additionally, A's histogram visualization includes shaded bands for ±1σ and ±2σ ranges overlaid on the chart, which better captures the intent of 'show the mean, median, and +/- 1 and 2 standard deviations' as a visual analysis tool rather than just numeric labels. B compensates with a more interactive UI (dynamic add/remove variables), but that feature wasn't requested and doesn't offset the missing Arbitrary variable type. A also uses a proper class hierarchy (Variable base class with subclasses) which aligns better with 'create reusable components, functions and classes'.

For the core simulation logic, variation-b (A) is more concise. Its variable factories use a functional pattern (e.g., `export const uniform = (min, max) => ({ sample: () => ... })`) that requires ~3-5 lines per distribution vs variation-c (B)'s class-based approach requiring ~7-9 lines each (constructor, this-assignments, method declaration) — 29 lines vs 57 lines for the same 5 distributions. The class boilerplate in B exists only to hold state that the closure in A captures for free. The stats module in A (30 lines) is also slightly heavier due to the `summarize` wrapper, but that wrapper is genuinely useful rather than incidental. The UI layer in A is larger and more abstracted (el/svgEl helpers, VARIABLE_TYPES config object, component factories), but this reflects a more complete feature implementation rather than incidental complexity. On balance, A introduces fewer constructs that exist only to support other constructs.
